name: Philippine Coconut Lowlands (Quezon)

staple_foods.name: coconut oil (lowland)

primary_metabolites.lauric_acid: 45.1-47.5% of total fatty acids [Cocos nucifera, Philippine lowland — Mansor et al. 2012 J Oleo Sci 61:735; sea-level humid lowland production]
primary_metabolites.myristic_acid: 16.8-18.2% of total fatty acids [Philippine coconut oil — Mansor et al. 2012]
primary_metabolites.oleic_acid: 5.8-7.2% of total fatty acids [Philippine coconut oil — Mansor et al. 2012; low oleic typical of lowland coconut]
primary_metabolites.linoleic_acid: 1.8-2.5% of total fatty acids [Philippine coconut oil — Mansor et al. 2012]

key_flavor_bioactives.polyphenol_content: 55-90 mg GAE/100g [virgin coconut oil — Arunima & Rajamohan 2013 Food Funct 4:270; polyphenols higher in VCO vs refined]
key_flavor_bioactives.tocopherol_content: 0.5-0.9 mg/100g [coconut oil tocopherols — Mansor et al. 2012; very low vs other plant oils]

staple_foods.notes: Philippine lowland coconut oil — warm, humid, sea-level growing conditions. Lauric acid (45-47%) is the defining saturated fat of coconut oil and varies with altitude and temperature. Natural experiment pair with RSU-33 (Sri Lankan Highland coconut, ~500m) — same species, contrasting altitude and temperature regime, allowing fatty acid composition comparison as a function of altitude/temperature gradient.
data_sources: Mansor et al. (2012) — Physicochemical properties of virgin coconut oil, J Oleo Sci 61:735
Arunima & Rajamohan (2013) — Virgin coconut oil and polyphenol content, Food Funct 4:270

staple_foods.name: rice (sinandomeng)
staple_foods.notes: White rice is the foundational carbohydrate of the Philippine diet. Sinandomeng is the dominant traditional lowland variety in Quezon province. FDC 168878 used as macro proxy.

staple_foods.name: tilapia (fresh, lake-raised)

umami_compounds.glutamate: ~0.28 g/100g free glutamate [tilapia muscle — Yoneda et al. 2004 estimate for freshwater fish]
umami_compounds.inosinate: ~0.25 g/100g IMP [tilapia — typical teleost muscle IMP; Maga 1983]

staple_foods.notes: Freshwater tilapia from Laguna de Bay — the largest lake in the Philippines and a major aquaculture center. Central protein source of Quezon and surrounding lowland communities.
data_sources: Maga (1983) — Flavor potentiators, CRC Crit Rev Food Sci Nutr 18:231

staple_foods.name: bagoong (fermented shrimp paste)

primary_metabolites.protein_content: 18-22 g/100g [bagoong alamang — Olympia 1992 SEAFDEC Aquacult Dept Tech Rep]
primary_metabolites.amino_acid_content: free amino acids dramatically elevated by proteolysis during fermentation

umami_compounds.glutamate: 1.2-2.8 g/100g free glutamate [bagoong alamang — Nozaki et al. 1999 Biosci Biotechnol Biochem 63:1474; extremely high due to extended proteolysis]

staple_foods.notes: Bagoong alamang is a fermented paste of tiny shrimp (Acetes sp.) salt-cured for 30-90 days. The hallmark of Philippine fermentation — the high free glutamate from proteolysis makes it one of the most umami-concentrated foods in any Asian food system. Used as condiment and cooking ingredient. Analogous to Malaysian belacan, Thai kapi, and Vietnamese mam ruoc.
data_sources: Olympia (1992) — Fermented fish products in the Philippines, SEAFDEC Aquacult Dept Tech Rep
Nozaki et al. (1999) — Free amino acids in fermented shrimp paste, Biosci Biotechnol Biochem 63:1474

staple_foods.name: calamansi

primary_metabolites.ascorbic_acid: 37-52 mg/100g [Citrofortunella microcarpa juice — Gonzalez et al. 2011 Philipp J Sci 140:133]

key_flavor_bioactives.polyphenol_content: 180-280 mg GAE/100g [calamansi peel — Gonzalez et al. 2011]

terpenes.limonene: present — dominant terpene in calamansi peel EO
terpenes.linalool: present — secondary terpene of calamansi EO

organic_acids.citric_acid: 5.8-7.5 g/100g fresh juice [calamansi — Gonzalez et al. 2011; higher acidity than key lime]

staple_foods.notes: Calamansi (Philippine lime) is the defining aromatic souring agent of the Philippine food system — used in dipping sauces (sawsawan), marinades, and squeezed over most savory dishes. The intense citric acid concentration (5.8-7.5 g/100g) is among the highest of any citrus fruit. Essential counterpoint to the rich coconut fat and umami-heavy fermented shrimp paste in this food system.
data_sources: Gonzalez et al. (2011) — Physicochemical and phytochemical properties of calamansi, Philipp J Sci 140:133

notes: Quezon Province, Luzon, Philippines (~50m). Warm, humid lowland tropical environment on volcanic soils. This RSU provides the low-altitude anchor for the coconut oil fat natural experiment (RSU-32 lowland vs RSU-33 Sri Lankan highland). Coconut oil lauric acid and myristic acid are the primary fat terroir signals. Bagoong provides the highest free glutamate in the dataset by a large margin — a distinct fermentation-terroir signal.
